content.problemStatement:
Arya has a chessboard with NNN rows (numbered 111 through NNN) and NNN columns (numbered 111 through NNN); a square in row rrr and column ccc is denoted by (r,c)(r, c)(r,c).

Arya has already placed KKK rooks on the chessboard in such a way that no two rooks attack each other. Note that two rooks attack each other if they are in the same row or in the same column and there is no rook between them. She is busy learning how to tame a dragon right now, so she asked you to place as many other rooks as possible on the chessboard in such a way that afterwards, no two rooks on the chessboard attack each other. Help Arya and choose the positions of these rooks.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case starts with two integers N and K. N is the size of the square chessboard (rows and columns are numbered from 1 to N). K is the number of rooks already placed. The next K lines each contain two integers r and c denoting the row and column of an already placed rook. It is guaranteed that the given rooks do not attack each other.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a line starting with P, the maximum number of additional rooks that can be placed (P = N-K). Then output 2*P integers r1 c1 r2 c2 ... rP cP describing the positions of the new rooks. The rooks must be placed so that after adding them no two rooks share a row or a column. Any valid placement is accepted; the reference solution pairs free rows with free columns in increasing order.

sampleTestCases.explanation: First test case: free rows are 3,4 and free columns are 1,3, so we place rooks at (3,1) and (4,3). Second test case: no rooks initially, we can fill the main diagonal.

First case: only row 1 and column 1 are occupied, free rows 2-5 and free columns 2-5, paired in order. Second case: rooks at (2,3) and (3,1) block rows 2,3 and columns 1,3, leaving row 1 and column 2 free, so one rook at (1,2). Third case: rooks occupy rows 2,5 and columns 5,3, leaving rows 1,3,4 and columns 1,2,4; we place four rooks pairing them.
The board has a single cell, so we can place one rook at (1,1).

solution.approach: Because rooks attack only along their own row and column, at most one rook can occupy each row and each column. The initial K rooks already occupy K distinct rows and K distinct columns. Therefore the maximum total number of rooks is N. The remaining N-K rooks must be placed on the rows that are still free and on the columns that are still free. Any bijection between the set of free rows and the set of free columns yields a valid configuration. The simplest construction is to list the free rows in increasing order, list the free columns in increasing order, and pair the i-th free row with the i-th free column. This produces exactly N-K additional rooks and runs in linear time.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case (to scan rows and columns and to collect the free ones). The total over all test cases is O(sum of N).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) per test case for the boolean arrays that mark used rows and columns.

code.Python3:
import sys
def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.buffer.read().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it)); K = int(next(it))
        row_used = [False]*(N+1)
        col_used = [False]*(N+1)
        for __ in range(K):
            r = int(next(it)); c = int(next(it))
            row_used[r] = True
            col_used[c] = True
        P = N - K
        parts = [str(P)]
        r_idx = 1; c_idx = 1
        for __ in range(P):
            while row_used[r_idx]:
                r_idx += 1
            while col_used[c_idx]:
                c_idx += 1
            parts.append(str(r_idx))
            parts.append(str(c_idx))
            r_idx += 1
            c_idx += 1
        out_lines.append(' '.join(parts))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))
if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
